L1.area: JavaScript array concatenation and element copying logic

L2.description: A heap buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the array concatenation fast paths of a JavaScript engine. When an array is filled from prototypes, reentrancy during concatenation can mutate the array length, causing element-copying routines to write beyond the allocated buffer bounds, leading to potential remote code execution.

L3.area: Native integer and floating-point array concatenation fast paths in the JavaScript array runtime library
L3.description: A heap buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the array concatenation fast paths that handle native integer and floating-point arrays. When an array is populated via copy-from-prototype, reentrancy triggered during the concatenation operation can mutate the array's length, causing element-copying routines to write beyond the allocated heap buffer bounds, potentially enabling remote code execution.

ground_truth.reason: Array length mutation introduced by copy-from-prototype reentrancy can cause heap overflow in concat fast paths, as the element-copying routines use a stale length without checking whether the array was filled from prototypes.
